era_context: The mid-240s CE were a period of relative stability under Philip the Arab, who had recently become emperor. The Sasanid Empire under Shapur I was consolidating power. In China, the Three Kingdoms period continued with Wei dominance. Christianity was growing and beginning to attract imperial attention.

events.title: Philip the Arab consolidates power in his second year as emperor
events.description: Philip the Arab, who had become emperor in 244 CE after the assassination of Gordian III, continued to consolidate his power in 246 CE. He made military appointments, distributed donatives to the troops, and organized public spectacles to build support. Philip's reign was characterized by relative stability compared to the chaos that would follow in the 250s, but underlying economic and military pressures were building. Philip's reign would ultimately prove unsustainable.

sources.name: Historia Augusta, Life of Philip the Arab

events.certainty_note: Philip's consolidation of power is attested in sources, but specific events in 246 CE are not always clearly distinguished from those of adjacent years.

events.title: Shapur I becomes Sasanid King of Kings; begins consolidation of power
events.description: Shapur I became Sasanid King of Kings around 240–246 CE, following the death of his father Ardashir II. Shapur's accession marked the beginning of a period of Sasanid expansion and consolidation. In his early years, Shapur focused on strengthening the Sasanid administrative system and military, preparing for the aggressive campaigns against Rome that would begin in the 250s. Shapur's reign would become one of the most successful in Sasanid history.
key_figures: Shapur I (new Sasanid King of Kings)

sources.name: Shapur I's Res Gestae Divi Sapuris (trilingual inscription at Naqsh-i Rustam)

events.certainty_note: Shapur I's accession is attested in his own inscriptions, but the exact date is debated. Some sources place it around 240 CE, others around 246 CE. The dating is approximate.

events.title: Wei Dynasty continues dominance in northern China under Cao Rui
events.description: Cao Rui, the fourth emperor of the Wei Dynasty, continued his reign in 246 CE. Wei remained the dominant power in northern China, though the state faced internal challenges from court factionalism and the growing power of military generals. Cao Rui's reign was marked by cultural achievements and relative stability, but the seeds of Wei's eventual decline were being sown.

sources.name: Records of Wei (Wei Zhi), part of the Sanguozhi

events.certainty_note: Cao Rui's reign is well-documented in Chinese historical records.

events.title: Christian communities continue to grow across the Roman Empire
events.region: Roman Empire (widespread, especially North Africa and Asia Minor)
events.description: In 246 CE, Christian communities across the Roman Empire continued to grow, despite sporadic local persecutions. The Christian church was becoming increasingly organized, with bishops establishing hierarchies and theological standards. Christian intellectuals like Origen and Cyprian of Carthage were producing influential theological works. The growth of Christianity was beginning to attract the attention of pagan intellectuals and the imperial authorities.
key_figures: Cyprian of Carthage (Christian bishop and theologian)

sources.name: Cyprian of Carthage, Letters and treatises

events.certainty_note: Christian growth and theological developments are attested in contemporary sources, though specific events in 246 CE are not always clearly distinguished from those of adjacent years.

disconfirming_evidence: The Historia Augusta's account of Philip the Arab is considered unreliable by modern scholars. Specific events in 246 CE are poorly documented, and it is difficult to distinguish them from events in adjacent years. The dating of Shapur I's accession is debated; some sources place it earlier (c. 240 CE) or later (c. 250 CE) than 246 CE.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 246 CE is moderate but sparse. Roman sources are relatively abundant but often written decades later and contain legendary elements. Chinese sources are contemporary or near-contemporary and generally reliable. Sources for other regions are sparse. The Historia Augusta is a major source for this period but is notoriously unreliable. For this year, much of the historical record is reconstructed from broader patterns rather than specific documented events. The dating of Shapur I's accession is particularly uncertain, with scholarly consensus placing it somewhere in the 240s CE but without precise agreement on the year.

graph_edges.note: Philip's consolidation efforts in 246 ultimately failed to prevent his overthrow in 249
Shapur I's accession and consolidation in 246 led to his aggressive campaigns against Rome in 250
